task: Design a JSON schema for a multi-tenant SaaS billing system supporting usage-based and seat-based pricing

criteria.description: Evaluates whether the schema enforces tenant boundaries at the structural level through proper tenant-scoped entity design, preventing cross-tenant data leakage by construction rather than application-level filtering.; also: Evaluates whether tenant isolation mechanisms are consistently applied across pricing configuration and usage metering, ensuring that tenant-scoped pricing rules align with tenant-isolated usage event processing and aggregation patterns.
criteria.pass_condition: All core entities (customers, subscriptions, usage_events, invoices) must include tenant_id as first component of primary key or composite index, with minimum 3 tenant-scoped foreign key relationships demonstrating cascading isolation, and explicit tenant_id field constraints on all billable entities.

sub_attributes.description: Tenant ID integrated into primary key structure or leading composite index for all tenant-scoped entities
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if tenant_id is first component of composite primary key or has unique composite index with tenant_id leading for all core entities, 0.7 if tenant_id present but not structurally enforced in keys, 0.4 if tenant_id exists as regular field only, 0.0 if missing tenant scoping

sub_attributes.description: Foreign key relationships maintain tenant isolation through proper constraint design
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if 4+ entities show proper tenant-scoped foreign keys with explicit tenant_id matching constraints, 0.7 if 2-3 entities properly scoped, 0.4 if basic foreign keys present without tenant isolation, 0.0 if no tenant-aware relationship constraints

sub_attributes.description: Schema structure prevents cross-tenant queries by requiring tenant_id in WHERE clauses through index design
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if indexes designed to require tenant_id filtering (no global indexes without tenant scope), 0.7 if most indexes tenant-scoped with some exceptions, 0.4 if mixed tenant-scoped and global indexes, 0.0 if indexes allow efficient cross-tenant queries

pass_examples: Primary key structure like 'id: {tenant_id}-{uuid}' or composite index '(tenant_id, entity_id)' with all foreign keys including tenant validation
fail_examples: Global auto-incrementing IDs with tenant_id as separate field, allowing efficient queries across all tenants
criteria.research_basis: All queries must be tenant-scoped by construction with mandatory tenant_id filters... Schema prevents cross-tenant reads through database constraints and row-level security

criteria.description: Assesses whether usage event schema supports high-volume tenant-isolated metering with proper time-series partitioning, idempotency guarantees, and aggregation structures that maintain audit trails while enabling efficient billing calculations.; also: Evaluates multi-layer idempotency protection across API, database, and event processing layers to prevent duplicate billing
criteria.pass_condition: Usage events must include idempotency keys, timestamp-based partitioning hints, tenant-scoped event IDs, and support both raw event storage and pre-computed aggregation tables with minimum 2-level time bucketing (hourly/daily or daily/monthly).
pass_examples: usage_events table with (tenant_id, idempotency_key) unique constraint, partition_date field, and separate usage_aggregates table linking back to source events
fail_examples: Usage events with auto-incrementing global IDs, calculated billing amounts stored directly without preserving raw usage data
criteria.research_basis: Idempotent operations that can be applied multiple times without changing results, preventing duplicate payments and incorrect balances... Client-generated UUID idempotency keys stored with database unique constraints

criteria.description: Evaluates schema flexibility to support complex pricing scenarios including tiered pricing, overage charges, proration calculations, mid-cycle plan changes, and hybrid seat/usage models with proper abstraction layers.
criteria.pass_condition: Schema must support minimum 3 pricing model types (seat-based, usage-based, hybrid) with tenant-scoped pricing rules, plan versioning for mid-cycle changes, and tier/overage rule definitions that enable pricing changes without schema modifications.

sub_attributes.description: Schema supports multiple pricing model types through proper abstraction
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if schema supports 3+ distinct pricing models (seat, usage, hybrid) with polymorphic price calculation rules, 0.7 if supports 2 pricing types with extensibility, 0.4 if single pricing model with variants, 0.0 if rigid single pricing approach

sub_attributes.description: Flexible tier definitions and overage calculations without hardcoded business logic
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if tier boundaries and overage rates stored as configurable data with calculation metadata, 0.7 if basic tiering with some configuration flexibility, 0.4 if limited tier support, 0.0 if no tiered pricing capability

sub_attributes.description: Mid-cycle plan changes with proration calculation support and effective dating
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if plan entities include version fields, effective_date ranges, and proration calculation metadata, 0.7 if basic versioning without proration support, 0.4 if plan changes require full cycle restart, 0.0 if no plan change support

sub_attributes.description: Pricing changes achievable through data configuration rather than code changes
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if pricing rules stored as data with calculation engine metadata, 0.7 if mostly configuration-driven with some code dependencies, 0.4 if mixed code and configuration approach, 0.0 if pricing logic hardcoded in schema

pass_examples: Pricing plans with polymorphic rule tables, tier_definitions with usage_breakpoints, plan_versions with effective_date and proration_rules
fail_examples: Hardcoded pricing columns for specific plans, no support for mid-cycle changes or tiered pricing variations
criteria.research_basis: Rating engine support for tiered pricing, usage-based billing, volume discounts, and flexible pricing strategies... Supports graduated pricing with per-unit price changes across tiers and real-time blended calculations

criteria.description: Evaluates idempotency patterns for billing operations, proper handling of financial calculations through event sourcing, and comprehensive audit trail preservation for billing corrections, refunds, and financial reconciliation.; also: Assesses whether schema stores immutable usage events rather than calculated billing amounts to prevent financial data corruption
criteria.pass_condition: Schema must support idempotent billing operations with unique constraint enforcement, event-sourced financial transactions with immutable audit trails, and proper correction/refund workflows that preserve original transaction history.
pass_examples: Transaction events with idempotency_key constraints, correction_transactions table linking to original transactions, immutable payment_events with balance calculation
fail_examples: Mutable account_balance fields updated directly, billing operations without idempotency protection, corrections that overwrite original transaction data
criteria.research_basis: Immutable audit records with known state of truth for billing disputes... Immutable event streams with no edits or erasures, permanent record of all billable actions

criteria.description: Assesses inclusion of data security and audit patterns required for financial systems, including data classification fields, encryption indicators, and audit logging structures that support regulatory compliance frameworks.; also: Assesses whether billing operations integrity requirements align with compliance standards and external API integration patterns, ensuring that audit trails, data classification, and webhook events maintain consistency across operational and compliance domains.
criteria.pass_condition: Schema must include data classification fields (e.g., 'data_type', 'sensitivity_level'), encryption requirement indicators for sensitive fields, geographic data placement fields for residency requirements, and audit logging tables with mandatory timestamp, user_id, action_type, and affected_entity tracking for all data modifications.

sub_attributes.description: Sensitive data properly classified with encryption requirements specified
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if payment/PII fields include classification metadata and encryption requirements, 0.7 if basic data sensitivity indicators present, 0.4 if limited classification scheme, 0.0 if no data classification

sub_attributes.description: Comprehensive audit logging for all data access and modification operations
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if dedicated audit tables capture all CRUD operations with user/timestamp tracking, 0.7 if audit logging for critical operations, 0.4 if basic change tracking, 0.0 if no audit logging structure

sub_attributes.description: Schema supports data residency requirements and regional data placement
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if tenant entities include data_region fields with region-specific constraints, 0.7 if basic regional support, 0.4 if limited geography awareness, 0.0 if no data residency consideration

sub_attributes.description: Payment data handling follows PCI DSS requirements with proper tokenization
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if payment methods use tokenization with external vault references, 0.7 if encrypted payment data with proper key management hints, 0.4 if basic payment data protection, 0.0 if raw payment data storage

pass_examples: Payment methods with vault_token references, audit_log table with operation tracking, data_classification enum fields on sensitive columns
fail_examples: Raw credit card numbers in schema, no audit trail for data access, missing data classification for PII fields
criteria.research_basis: PCI DSS for payment card data security and SOC 2 for broader data protection, with about 60% overlap in requirements including access control, encryption, and vendor management

criteria.description: Evaluates database schema design for high-volume billing operations with proper indexing for time-series queries, partitioning strategies for tenant data, and query optimization patterns for long-term performance.; also: Evaluates whether financial calculation accuracy requirements are maintained under high-performance scalability patterns, ensuring that partitioning strategies and aggregation optimizations preserve decimal precision and audit trail completeness.
criteria.pass_condition: Schema must include explicit partitioning hints for time-series data, optimized index strategies for tenant-scoped queries, and data archival patterns that maintain performance under high volume.

sub_attributes.description: Optimized indexing strategies for time-based billing queries
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if explicit index hints for time-range queries with tenant scoping, 0.7 if basic time-based indexing, 0.4 if limited temporal optimization, 0.0 if no time-series index strategy

sub_attributes.description: Data partitioning patterns for tenant and temporal data distribution
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if schema includes partitioning metadata for both tenant and time-based distribution, 0.7 if basic partitioning hints, 0.4 if limited partitioning strategy, 0.0 if no partitioning consideration

sub_attributes.description: Schema design supports efficient common billing query patterns
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if index design optimizes for tenant billing cycles and usage aggregation queries, 0.7 if good query optimization for most patterns, 0.4 if basic optimization, 0.0 if no query pattern consideration

sub_attributes.description: Schema supports data lifecycle management and archival strategies
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if entities include lifecycle metadata and archival status indicators, 0.7 if basic archival support, 0.4 if limited lifecycle management, 0.0 if no archival strategy

pass_examples: Index definitions prioritizing (tenant_id, created_date) patterns, partition_key fields on high-volume tables, archive_status enum fields
fail_examples: Generic indexing without tenant optimization, no consideration for data growth patterns, missing archival lifecycle management
criteria.research_basis: [Partial] Performance issues in shared databases as tenants grow due to poor indexing strategies for high-volume usage event data

criteria.description: Evaluates proper domain separation and entity relationship modeling following telecommunications industry patterns for customer, product, and usage data organization.
criteria.pass_condition: Schema must implement TMF632 Party Management, TMF620 Product Catalog, and TMF635 Usage Management API data models with proper entity inheritance hierarchies, standardized identifier patterns (party.id, product.id, usage.id), and TMF-compliant relationship cardinalities between Customer, Product, and UsageSpecification entities.

sub_attributes.description: Uses TM Forum SID core entities (Customer, Product, Usage, Billing Account)
Proper separation between customer management, product catalog, and usage domains
Follows SID relationship patterns between entities

pass_examples: Schema includes separate Customer, BillingAccount, ProductOffering, and UsageRecord entities with proper foreign key relationships
Clear separation between customer data and billing data following SID patterns
fail_examples: Flat schema mixing customer and billing data without domain separation
Custom entity naming that doesn't align with SID standards
criteria.research_basis: TM Forum SID provides an information/data reference model along with a common vocabulary for implementing business processes, independent of platform, language or protocol
